auctions.autopsy: Healthy demand for 364-day bills with bid-cover at 1.75x. The 11bps yield decline reflects rising banking liquidity from BB's dollar purchases. No devolvement signals comfortable primary dealer appetite.
Strong demand for the short end with bid-cover nearly 2x. Banks are aggressively parking excess liquidity in the safest tenor. The 10bps decline is consistent with the broader easing trend.
Moderate demand for 10Y bonds. The 200 Cr devolvement (10%) suggests some primary dealer reluctance at these levels, though the yield compression continues. Duration risk appetite remains measured.
Solid demand for the belly of the curve. Banks prefer the 5Y point for a balance of carry and manageable duration risk. No devolvement indicates comfortable market clearing.

The 182-day T-bill auction cleared at a cutoff of 10.20% with a bid-cover of 1.60x, reflecting adequate but unspectacular demand — the full notified amount was absorbed without devolvement, though the relatively modest oversubscription suggests primary dealers are not aggressively chasing duration even at the short end, likely reflecting cautious liquidity management amid elevated rates across the curve. The tight 5bp spread between the weighted average yield of 10.15% and the cutoff signals reasonably competitive bidding within accepted tenders, though the clustering implies limited price discovery dispersion and a market broadly in agreement on fair value for the tenor. Notably, the 182-day cutoff at 10.20% sits above the 364-day yield of 10.07%, creating a localized curve inversion between the two tenors that points to distorted supply-demand dynamics or market expectation of near-term easing beyond the six-month horizon — a signal worth monitoring as it suggests participants are pricing in some degree of policy pivot or improved liquidity conditions in the back half of the year. Near-term, the absence of devolvement and the clean auction print provide modest comfort, but the tepid bid-cover and persistent belly inversion suggest the market remains in a cautious equilibrium, and any uptick in government borrowing or tightening in system liqu

The 91D T-Bill auction cleared at a cutoff of 10.05%, marking a 20bp cheapening from the previous auction's 9.85% cutoff, signaling a notable repricing at the front end as market participants demand incrementally higher compensation amid tightening liquidity conditions. The bid-cover ratio softened to 1.68x from 1.92x in the prior round, suggesting a meaningful deterioration in demand quality — while still above the critical 1.5x threshold, the compression in oversubscription indicates waning enthusiasm from primary dealers likely contending with balance sheet constraints or repositioning expectations around near-term policy direction. The full acceptance of the notified BDT 2500 Cr without devolvement is a modest positive, though the declining appetite and rising yields suggest Bangladesh Bank may face increasing resistance at subsequent auctions if systemic liquidity remains under pressure. Looking ahead, the sharp front-end selloff — particularly notable given the inverted 91D-364D spread where 91D now trades essentially flat to the 364D at 10.07% — points to potential further bear-flattening of the short end and raises the probability that upcoming auctions across tenors will need to concede additional yield to attract sufficient demand.
Weak demand for ultra-long duration. Bid-cover at just 1.1x with 25% devolvement signals reluctance from PDs to lock in 20Y exposure at current yields. The long end remains illiquid.

The 364-day T-bill auction cleared at a cutoff of 10.32%, marking a notable 25bp cheapening from the previous auction's 10.07%, while the bid-cover ratio softened to 1.50x from 1.75x, signaling a tangible deterioration in demand quality and suggesting the market is repricing the short end higher amid tightening liquidity conditions or shifting rate expectations. The tight 4bp spread between the weighted average yield of 10.28% and the cutoff indicates relatively concentrated bidding near the marginal rate, implying dealers had reasonable consensus on fair value but were unwilling to reach aggressively — a classic signal of cautious positioning rather than distressed demand. The full subscription at notified amount without devolvement provides some comfort, though the declining cover ratio trajectory warrants monitoring as further erosion toward 1.2-1.3x territory would materially increase devolvement risk for primary dealers in subsequent rounds. With the 364-day now printing at 10.32% versus the 2-year at 11.95% — a 163bp term premium that remains steep — and the belly of the curve (5Y at 10.82%) compressing toward the new 364-day level, the near-term signal is decisively bearish for the front end, and market participants should position for continued upward pressure on short
